does: This paper establishes that three major adaptation mechanisms for large language models—parameter updates through fine-tuning, reward model optimization, and in-context prompting—are fundamentally interchangeable and convertible into one another. The authors construct a triangular framework showing six bidirectional transformation pathways between these three approaches, demonstrating that techniques developed within one adaptation paradigm can be translated to and applied within another. The work provides concrete examples and theoretical grounding for how information and capabilities encoded through one method can be transferred or reproduced using the other two, effectively showing these are different interfaces to the same underlying adaptation problem.
connects: This work builds on decades of LLM adaptation research spanning multiple subfields. It synthesizes classical parameter fine-tuning methods developed since the transformer era, reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) and reward modeling approaches popularized by recent instruction-tuned systems like ChatGPT, and in-context learning discoveries showing LLMs can adapt through examples without gradient updates. The paper responds to the fragmentation of these communities by recognizing they address the same underlying problem through different mechanisms, building on unification efforts in meta-learning and prompt engineering while extending insights from equivalence results in other ML domains.
enables: This framework opens pathways for cross-pollinating techniques across adaptation communities—methods developed for efficient fine-tuning could inspire new prompting strategies, while reward modeling insights could improve parameter update schedules. It suggests new research directions in understanding the fundamental limits and complementarities of each approach, potentially leading to hybrid adaptation systems that combine all three mechanisms. The work enables principled approaches to selecting adaptation methods for specific constraints, developing universal translation algorithms between paradigms, and building more interpretable adaptation systems by understanding the common computation underlying these seemingly different techniques.
